title: Template-driven artifact architecture — skeleton spec
topic: Vector's pivot to template-driven artifacts with cell-level visibility, search-and-compose authoring, and bulk reuse
summary: Skeleton design spec for Vector's template-driven artifact system — three-table pattern (artifact + template_forms + field_values), cell-level visibility (0–3), search/reuse, dual-run migration.
content:
1. Overview

This spec captures Vector's architectural pivot from rigid, column-locked tables (e.g. portfolio_items, user_stories) to a template-driven artifact model where:

  • Each artifact type has a small core table with bare-minimum fields (id, title, content, template_form_id, subscription_id, audit columns).
  • Each artifact type has a paired _template_forms table that defines reusable form templates — sets of additional fields that teams or padmins compose for their context.
  • All template-driven field data lives in a paired _field_values table with a cell-level visibility flag (0=private, 1=product, 2=workspace, 3=tenant) — enabling fine-grained governance, audit, and partition without RBAC gymnastics.
  • Authoring is search-and-compose: when a user fills out a story, they can search across all artifacts they have visibility into and copy field content into the new artifact (copies, not links — preserves tailoring).
  • Bulk operations let teams apply field content (e.g. acceptance criteria) to many artifacts at once.

The system is built alongside the existing schema. Old tables stay live; new tables are populated through new UI pages. Once validated, data migrates and old tables retire.

2. Why This Architecture

2.0 Foundational principle: open source first, no subscription costs

Hard rule: Vector commits to an open-source-first stack with no per-customer subscription costs to upstream vendors. The order of preference for any external dependency is: (1) MIT/BSD/Apache-licensed open source, (2) self-hosted open core, (3) build our own, (4) paid SaaS as last resort only. This is why Lexical was chosen over Tiptap (Tiptap has a paid commercial tier; Lexical does not), Craft.js over BuilderIO, self-hosted Postgres FTS over Algolia. Subscription costs compound forever; one-time build effort amortises. The project has unlimited time horizon and is hobby-funded, which permanently reframes "ship fast and pay vendors" into "ship right and own the stack."

2.1 Business benefits

  • Infinite extensibility without code: customers compose their own artifact shapes. No deploy needed.
  • Subscription tiering by feature: sell execution layer, strategic layer, testing container as separate SKUs. Add template packs as upsells.
  • Compliance as data, not code: healthcare, fintech, govtech ship as template packs. One codebase, infinite verticals.
  • Future-proof against framework drift: Agile evolves (SAFe, OKRs, AI workflows). Publish new templates instead of refactoring.
  • Team autonomy at scale: different product silos within one subscription get different templates without forking.
  • Catalog effect: every artifact written becomes reusable institutional knowledge. Lock-in via accumulated org memory.

2.2 Architectural benefits

  • Schema is stable — feature growth happens in data, not DDL.
  • Cell-level visibility unifies governance across all artifact types (one mechanism, infinite applications).
  • Search index is uniform: every artifact and field has a searchable text column; one query language for all reuse.

3. Core Schema Pattern

Every artifact type follows the same three-table pattern, named consistently:

artefacts_<domain>_<type>                 -- core artifact rows
artefacts_<domain>_<type>_template_forms  -- template form definitions
artefacts_<domain>_<type>_field_values    -- per-row dynamic field data

Plus a fourth supporting table per type:

artefacts_<domain>_<type>_template_form_fields  -- relational fields per template

3.0 Templates are form definitions, not content containers

Hard rule: a template defines the shape of an artefact (which fields exist, their types, their default visibility). It does not define content. Templates are also locked to a single artefact type — a user-story template lives in artefacts_execution_user_stories_template_forms and is forever a user-story form. There is no template inheritance, no cross-type extension, no parent/child relationship between templates. A "Compliance Story" template is a standalone duplicate of a "Standard Story" template; once forked, the two evolve independently. This is intentional — drift between forks is a feature (different teams, different needs), not a bug.

Cross-pollination happens at the content layer, not the template layer. The search-and-compose authoring engine (section 5) lets users search across _field_values rows from any artefact they can see, and copy field content into the artefact they're editing. The template is the container; the values are the reusable currency.

3.1 Core artifact table (example: user stories)

CREATE TABLE artefacts_execution_user_stories (
    id                  UUID            PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    subscription_id     UUID            NOT NULL REFERENCES subscriptions(id) ON DELETE RESTRICT,
    key_num             BIGINT          NOT NULL,        -- per-subscription sequence (US-00000001 etc.)
    title               TEXT            NOT NULL,
    content             JSONB,                           -- Lexical rich-text state (replaces "description")
    content_plain_text  TEXT,                            -- denormalised plain text for FTS and previews
    template_form_id    UUID            REFERENCES artefacts_execution_user_stories_template_forms(id),
    visibility          SMALLINT        NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,  -- 0/1/2/3 row-level default
    visibility_scope_id UUID,                            -- FK to product/workspace scope
    created_by          UUID            NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE RESTRICT,
    created_at          TIMESTAMPTZ     NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    updated_at          TIMESTAMPTZ     NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    archived_at         TIMESTAMPTZ,
    search_index        TSVECTOR         -- full-text search
);

The core table holds only fields universal to every instance of that artifact type. Anything optional, contextual, or template-specific lives in _field_values.

3.2 Template forms table

CREATE TABLE artefacts_execution_user_stories_template_forms (
    id                  UUID            PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    subscription_id     UUID            NOT NULL REFERENCES subscriptions(id) ON DELETE RESTRICT,
    name                TEXT            NOT NULL,        -- "Standard Story", "Spike", "Compliance Story"
    description         TEXT,
    visibility          SMALLINT        NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,  -- who can use this template
    visibility_scope_id UUID,
    created_by          UUID            NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE RESTRICT,
    created_at          TIMESTAMPTZ     NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    updated_at          TIMESTAMPTZ     NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    archived_at         TIMESTAMPTZ
);

3.3 Template form fields (relational)

CREATE TABLE artefacts_execution_user_stories_template_form_fields (
    id                  UUID            PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    template_form_id    UUID            NOT NULL REFERENCES artefacts_execution_user_stories_template_forms(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    field_name          TEXT            NOT NULL,        -- internal key (e.g. acceptance_criteria)
    field_label         TEXT            NOT NULL,        -- display text
    field_type          TEXT            NOT NULL,        -- text|number|boolean|date|select|multiselect|jsonb
    required            BOOLEAN         NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,
    position            INTEGER         NOT NULL,
    default_visibility  SMALLINT        NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,  -- default cell visibility for this field
    options_json        JSONB,                          -- vocab list for select/multiselect
    config_json         JSONB,                          -- per-type config (validation, regex, min/max)
    created_at          TIMESTAMPTZ     NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    UNIQUE (template_form_id, field_name)
);

3.4 Field values (with cell-level visibility)

CREATE TABLE artefacts_execution_user_stories_field_values (
    id                  UUID            PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    artefact_id         UUID            NOT NULL REFERENCES artefacts_execution_user_stories(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    template_field_id   UUID            REFERENCES artefacts_execution_user_stories_template_form_fields(id),
    field_name          TEXT            NOT NULL,        -- denormalised for query
    value_text          TEXT,
    value_number        NUMERIC(19,4),
    value_boolean       BOOLEAN,
    value_date          DATE,
    value_jsonb         JSONB,
    visibility          SMALLINT        NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,  -- THE cell-level flag
    visibility_scope_id UUID,
    source_artefact_id  UUID,                           -- audit: where this content came from
    subscription_id     UUID            NOT NULL REFERENCES subscriptions(id) ON DELETE RESTRICT,
    created_by          UUID            NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE RESTRICT,
    created_at          TIMESTAMPTZ     NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    updated_at          TIMESTAMPTZ     NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    search_index        TSVECTOR
);

Notes:

  • visibility on the value row overrides any default — cell-level wins.
  • source_artefact_id records where copied content originated (for audit/provenance), without enforcing a relationship.
  • Typed value columns (value_text/value_number/etc.) — exactly one is non-NULL per row.

4. Cell-Level Visibility Model

Every cell carries its own visibility flag. Default policy: private (0) unless explicitly elevated.

FlagLevelWho can seeUse case
0PrivateAuthor only (or their team if scoped)Drafts, sensitive AC, internal risk notes
1ProductThe product/vertical siloClient API endpoints, team-wide context
2WorkspaceEntire subscriptionOrg-wide standards, shared templates
3TenantCross-subscription (if multi-tenant later)Public template packs, vendor SDK fields

4.1 Query pattern

When fetching an artifact, the backend filters cells by the requesting user's effective visibility level:

SELECT field_name, value_text
FROM artefacts_execution_user_stories_field_values
WHERE artefact_id = $1
  AND visibility <= $user_max_visibility
  AND subscription_id = $user_subscription_id;

The row exists for everyone, but invisible cells are stripped server-side. UI sees a partial artifact and renders accordingly (greyed-out fields, or hidden entirely).

4.2 Search filter

Cross-artifact search must also respect visibility — users only find what they can see:

SELECT artefact_id, field_name, ts_headline(value_text, query)
FROM artefacts_execution_user_stories_field_values
WHERE search_index @@ to_tsquery($search_term)
  AND visibility <= $user_max_visibility
  AND subscription_id = $user_subscription_id;

5. Search-and-Compose Authoring

The signature UX move: artifact authoring is search-driven. When a user opens a new story:

  1. Base fields render (title, content, template choice).
  2. A search bar offers cross-artifact lookup ("find acceptance criteria, requirements, past stories...").
  3. Click a result → copy the cell text into the new artifact's field. Tailor as needed.
  4. Save → new field value row, with source_artefact_id pointing at the origin.

5.1 Why copy, not link

Each artifact owns its content. Teams can adapt copied AC for context ("add 'in under 2 seconds'") without affecting the source. source_artefact_id preserves the breadcrumb but enforces no relationship.

5.2 Bulk operations

Teams select N artifacts → search a field → bulk-paste content. Implementation:

INSERT INTO artefacts_execution_user_stories_field_values
  (artefact_id, field_name, value_text, source_artefact_id, visibility, subscription_id, created_by)
SELECT unnest($artefact_ids::uuid[]), $field_name, $value_text, $source_id, $visibility, $sub_id, $user_id
ON CONFLICT (artefact_id, field_name) DO UPDATE
SET value_text = EXCLUDED.value_text,
    source_artefact_id = EXCLUDED.source_artefact_id,
    updated_at = now();

Wrapped in a transaction — partial failure rolls back the whole batch.

6. Phase 1 Schema — Execution + Strategic

6.1 Execution layer (4 artifact types)

Core tableTemplate formsTemplate fieldsField values
artefacts_execution_user_stories..._template_forms..._template_form_fields..._field_values
artefacts_execution_defects..._template_forms..._template_form_fields..._field_values
artefacts_execution_tasks..._template_forms..._template_form_fields..._field_values
artefacts_execution_test_cases..._template_forms..._template_form_fields..._field_values

6.2 Strategic layer (1 artifact type)

Core tableTemplate formsTemplate fieldsField values
artefacts_strategic..._template_forms..._template_form_fields..._field_values

6.3 Phase 1 totals

5 artifact types × 4 tables each = 20 tables in Phase 1, plus shared sequence/visibility infrastructure.

6.4 Core columns required on every artifact type

  • id (UUID PK)
  • subscription_id (tenant isolation)
  • key_num (per-subscription sequence)
  • title (NOT NULL)
  • content (rich text, nullable)
  • template_form_id (nullable — artifact may have no template)
  • visibility (SMALLINT, default 0)
  • visibility_scope_id (UUID, nullable)
  • created_by, created_at, updated_at, archived_at
  • search_index (TSVECTOR for full-text)

Per-type extensions live in _field_values.

7. Phase 2 Skeleton — Remaining Domains

Same three-table pattern across:

7.1 Artefacts (additional types)

  • artefacts_risk
  • artefacts_incidents
  • artefacts_scope_change
  • artefacts_feedback_user
  • artefacts_feedback_client
  • artefacts_feedback_stake_holder
  • artefacts_technical_requirements
  • artefacts_sme
  • artefacts_governance_requirements
  • artefacts_support_tickets
  • artefacts_health_scores

7.2 Facilitation (session records)

  • facilitation_session_stand_up
  • facilitation_session_retrospective
  • facilitation_session_review
  • facilitation_session_refinement
  • facilitation_session_planning
  • facilitation_session_estimation
  • facilitation_session_demo
  • facilitation_session_technical_spike
  • facilitation_session_bug_triage
  • facilitation_session_security_review
  • facilitation_session_compliance_review
  • facilitation_session_post_mortems
  • facilitation_session_governance

7.3 Timebox (planning containers)

  • timebox_sprints
  • timebox_releases
  • timebox_pi (Program Increments)
  • timebox_milestones
  • Waterfall phases: timebox_waterfall_phase, _requirements, _implementation, _testing, _deployment, _maintenance, _start_up, _initiation, _stage_delivery, _product_delivery, _closing_out

7.4 Testing container

  • testing_test_case
  • testing_test_type
  • testing_test_group
  • testing_test_plan
  • testing_test_profile

7.5 Phase 2 totals

~40 additional artifact types × 4 tables each = ~160 tables in Phase 2.

Sounds large but it's mechanical — every table follows the identical three-table pattern. A code generator can scaffold them all from a YAML manifest. Schema migrations become declarative.

8. Search Strategy

8.1 Per-table TSVECTOR (Phase 1 default)

Every core table and every _field_values table has a search_index TSVECTOR column maintained by trigger. Cross-artifact search is a UNION across all _field_values tables, filtered by visibility and subscription.

CREATE TRIGGER trg_artefacts_execution_user_stories_search
BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE ON artefacts_execution_user_stories
FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION update_search_index();

8.2 Unified search view (Phase 2 option)

For global search performance, build a materialised view consolidating all _field_values rows into one searchable surface, refreshed on commit or via background job. This avoids N-table UNION queries when the user searches across artifact types.

8.3 Future: external index (Phase 3)

If Postgres FTS becomes a bottleneck, mirror to Meilisearch or Elasticsearch. Same visibility filter applies — index documents are tagged with visibility/subscription, queries filter accordingly.

9. Migration Path (Dual-Run)

9.1 Old schema stays live

Tables portfolio_items, portfolio_item_types, item_field_definitions, item_field_values, item_field_options remain untouched. Existing pages, services, and APIs continue working.

9.2 New schema added in parallel

Migrations 053+ create the new artefacts_*, facilitation_*, timebox_*, testing_* tables alongside the old ones. They share the same database; isolation is by table name.

9.3 New UI pages

Duplicate the current portfolio page → create a new page (e.g. /p/strategic-artefacts or /dev/template-forms) that reads from and writes to the new tables. Old page keeps the old behaviour.

9.4 Validation period

Run both side-by-side. Build templates, create artifacts, test bulk operations, search, visibility filtering. Iterate on the schema if needed (it's still in dev, not customer-visible).

9.5 Cutover

When confident:

  1. Migrate existing portfolio_items rows to artefacts_strategic + _field_values (custom fields become field values; standard columns become standard fields in a default template).
  2. Migrate user_stories rows to artefacts_execution_user_stories + _field_values.
  3. Update old API routes to read from new tables (or proxy through).
  4. Drop old pages, then old tables.

9.6 Rollback

If the pivot fails, the old tables are still live and authoritative. Drop the new tables, scrap the new pages. Zero data loss because old data was never deleted during validation.

10. Backend API Patterns

10.1 Read

GET /api/artefacts/execution/user-stories/:id
→ Returns: {
    id, subscription_id, key_num, title, content,
    template_form_id, visibility, archived_at, ...,
    fields: [
      { field_name, value, visibility, source_artefact_id }
      // only fields the requesting user can see
    ],
    template_form: {
      id, name, fields: [ /* template definition */ ]
    }
  }

10.2 Search

GET /api/artefacts/search?q=login+flow&types=user_stories,defects&field_names=acceptance_criteria
→ Returns: [
    { artefact_id, type, title, field_name, snippet, visibility }
  ]
  // visibility-filtered, subscription-scoped

10.3 Bulk apply

POST /api/artefacts/execution/user-stories/bulk-apply
Body: {
  artefact_ids: [uuid, uuid, ...],
  field_name: 'acceptance_criteria',
  value_text: '...',
  source_artefact_id: uuid,
  visibility: 0
}
→ Atomic batch insert/update across N rows.

10.4 Visibility filtering at the service layer

Service layer computes user_max_visibility from role + subscription policy + scope memberships. Every read query joins/filters by it. UI never sees the raw row — only sanitised projections.

11. PoS+ (Point of Sale Plus) Implications

11.1 Tier strategy

TierVisibility capsTemplate countSearch scopeBulk ops
Free0 only (private)3 templatesPer-artifact only—
Pro0–1 (product)20 templatesCross-artifact within type10 rows max
Team0–2 (workspace)UnlimitedCross-type100 rows max
Enterprise0–3 (tenant)Unlimited + custom packsCross-type + tenantUnlimited

11.2 Template marketplace

Pre-built template packs for verticals (Healthcare, Fintech, Govtech), frameworks (SAFe, OKRs, Scrum-at-Scale), or workflows (DevSecOps, MLOps, Agile-Marketing). Each pack is a JSON bundle of _template_forms + _template_form_fields rows. Customers install with one click.

11.3 Template authoring as service

Vector consultancy: design custom templates for enterprise customers, charge per pack. The schema makes this scalable — no code changes per customer.

12. Open Questions & Gaps

  • Visibility scope_id semantics: exactly which entity does visibility_scope_id reference? Likely a products (vertical) or workspaces table not yet defined. Need to design this before Phase 1 implementation.
  • Effective user visibility computation: how is user_max_visibility derived? Probably user.role + user.product_membership + subscription.policy. Spec needs writing.
  • Cross-artifact links: when a user story references a portfolio artifact (parent), do we keep a typed FK column on the core table, or generalise into a polymorphic artefact_links table? Recommend the latter for consistency.
  • Computed/rollup fields: count_child_*, done_by_*, count_rollup_* from R007/R008 — do these become field values (computed and stored) or computed views? Likely materialised views.
  • Comments & discussions: RESOLVED — Q5 = D. Comments are a page-builder block type, backed by page_blocks + page_block_comments. Same model serves artefact pages, intranet pages, dashboards, retros. See section 14.
  • Template inheritance: RESOLVED — Q6 = A. No inheritance. Templates are standalone form definitions, locked to a single artefact type. Forks are full copies that evolve independently. Cross-pollination is at the content (field-value) layer via search-and-compose, not the template layer. See section 3.0.
  • Field type plugins: RESOLVED — Q7 = D + Lexical rich text. Phase 1 ships 7 standard types hardcoded (text, number, boolean, date, select, multiselect, jsonb) plus a Lexical-based WYSIWYG editor for the artefact content column. Lexical (Meta, MIT licence, no commercial tier) chosen over Tiptap to avoid freemium-ladder lock-in — Vector commits to open-source-first, no-subscription-cost stack. Storage: content JSONB (Lexical state) + content_plain_text TEXT (denormalised for FTS and previews). The field_type TEXT column and a FieldRenderer registry exist from day one so Phase 2 can register additional types (rich-text fields, user picker, file attachment, formula, lookup, URL, colour, currency, duration, geo) without schema changes.
  • Sequence allocation: RESOLVED — Q8 = C. Two-table registry pattern separates the immutable internal scope key from the user-mutable display prefix. artefact_type_registry(scope_key PK, artefact_table, default_prefix, display_label, description) is system-owned and seeded by migrations — one row per artefact type, scope_key matches the table name (e.g. 'execution_user_stories'). subscription_artefact_type_overrides(subscription_id, scope_key, display_prefix, display_label) stores per-subscription padmin renames (e.g. 'US' → 'STORY' → 'GO'). Code references scope_key (stable); UI renders display_prefix (mutable). The underlying data only stores (scope_key, key_num), never the rendered string, so tag renames are instant row updates with no sequence migration. Adding a new artefact type in Phase 2 = add a registry row + create three tables, no special-case logic.
  • Search index maintenance: RESOLVED — Q9 = B + pgvector overlay. Async worker pattern: Postgres triggers queue (artefact_id, table_name) to a NOTIFY channel on every write; a Go worker LISTENs, recomputes TSVECTOR + fetches embedding from a self-hosted Ollama/sentence-transformers service, UPDATEs both columns. Writes stay fast; index is eventually-consistent (sub-second). pgvector overlay added from Phase 1 — every _field_values table gets a content_embedding vector(N) column with an HNSW index. Search bar is hybrid lexical (TSVECTOR) + semantic (vector cosine) from launch. See section 14.
  • Backup & restore granularity (RESOLVED — Option D, hybrid): two distinct concerns, two mechanisms. (1) Disaster recovery — Postgres point-in-time recovery (PITR) via WAL archiving plus nightly pg_dump to S3-compatible storage. Handles "DB exploded" / "roll the tenant back to last Tuesday". Free with Postgres, no app code. (2) User-facing undo / version history — per-artefact version snapshots in an artefact_versions table written on every UPDATE; archived_at already provides soft-delete recovery. Surfaces a Rally-style version history panel and per-artefact restore. (3) Per-cell audit log — explicitly deferred; the version table contains the data needed to denormalise into a field-change log on demand if a compliance customer (SOC2, GDPR DSR) ever asks. Cell-level visibility leak risk on restore is mitigated by the visibility flag travelling with each cell inside the snapshot — a restored cell carries its original visibility, never leaks upward.

13. Page Builder Architecture (Elementor-style)

14.1 Strategic intent

Vector commits to a WordPress/Elementor-style page builder as a core engine, not an add-on. Every composable surface in the product — artefact detail pages, team intranets, stakeholder dashboards, custom pages, facilitation boards — renders through one block-composition layer. Build the engine once, surface it everywhere.

14.2 Runtime: Craft.js

After evaluating GrapesJS, Craft.js, Puck, BuilderIO/builder, and a custom-build option, Craft.js is selected. Rationale:

  • React-native, TypeScript-first — no foreign DOM model to bridge. Existing Vector components (ChartWidget, charts, tables, headers) wrap as blocks with ~20 lines each.
  • Designed for embedding — Craft was built specifically for "build your own page builder", not as a hosted product.
  • JSON serialisation built-in — page state is a tree of nodes, persisted directly to the database. No custom serialiser.
  • Dual-mode rendering — same component renders read-only on a published page and editable in builder mode, gated by a single context flag.
  • MIT licence, no vendor lock-in — unlike BuilderIO which has a hosted upsell path.
  • Plugin/marketplace fit — a block is a registered React component + manifest JSON. Selling block packs in PoS+ is a clean architectural fit.

GrapesJS was the runner-up but is built around HTML/CSS DOM trees rather than React component trees, making live-data blocks (e.g. burndown charts wired to APIs) awkward.

14.3 Core schema — universal block layer

CREATE TABLE page_blocks (
    id                  UUID            PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    subscription_id     UUID            NOT NULL REFERENCES subscriptions(id) ON DELETE RESTRICT,
    host_kind           TEXT            NOT NULL,        -- 'artefact' | 'custom_page' | 'dashboard' | 'intranet' | 'facilitation'
    host_id             UUID            NOT NULL,        -- polymorphic FK to host (cleanup via entityrefs)
    parent_block_id     UUID            REFERENCES page_blocks(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,  -- nesting (rows/columns)
    block_type          TEXT            NOT NULL,        -- 'comments' | 'markdown' | 'field_group' | 'chart' | 'roster' | ...
    position            INTEGER         NOT NULL,
    config_json         JSONB,                           -- per-block settings (chart type, query, styling)
    visibility          SMALLINT        NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,  -- 0/1/2/3 — same flag as artefact cells
    visibility_scope_id UUID,
    created_by          UUID            NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE RESTRICT,
    created_at          TIMESTAMPTZ     NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    updated_at          TIMESTAMPTZ     NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    archived_at         TIMESTAMPTZ
);

CREATE TABLE page_block_comments (
    id                  UUID            PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    block_id            UUID            NOT NULL REFERENCES page_blocks(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    parent_comment_id   UUID            REFERENCES page_block_comments(id),  -- threading
    body                TEXT            NOT NULL,
    body_format         TEXT            NOT NULL DEFAULT 'markdown',
    author_id           UUID            NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE RESTRICT,
    visibility          SMALLINT        NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    subscription_id     UUID            NOT NULL REFERENCES subscriptions(id) ON DELETE RESTRICT,
    created_at          TIMESTAMPTZ     NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    updated_at          TIMESTAMPTZ     NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    edited_at           TIMESTAMPTZ,
    archived_at         TIMESTAMPTZ
);

14.4 Block type registry

Phase 1 ships a curated set of core blocks. Each block is a React component + manifest JSON declaring name, props schema, default config, required tier.

BlockSurfacesTier
TitleBlockartefact, page, dashboard, intranetFree
MarkdownBlockanyFree
FieldGroupBlockartefact (renders _field_values)Free
CommentsBlockanyFree
ActivityBlockartefact, intranetPro
BurndownBlockdashboard, intranetPro
VelocityBlockdashboard, intranetPro
BacklogTableBlockdashboard, intranet, custom pagePro
RosterBlockintranetPro
KpiBlockdashboardPro
ChartBlock (generic)anyPro
StickyNotesBlockfacilitationTeam
VotingBlockfacilitationTeam
Premium chart packs (sankey, sunburst, custom-axis)anyPoS+ pack
Vertical templates (Defence, NHS, Fintech)anyPoS+ pack
Integration blocks (Jira, GitHub, Slack)anyPoS+ pack

14.5 How surfaces collapse into one engine

Surfacehost_kindComposed of
Artefact detail pageartefactTitleBlock + FieldGroupBlock + CommentsBlock + ActivityBlock
Team intranetintranetTitleBlock + RosterBlock + BurndownBlock + BacklogTableBlock + MarkdownBlock
Stakeholder dashboarddashboardKpiBlock × N + ChartBlock × N + BacklogTableBlock
Custom page (/p/<uuid>)custom_pageany combination
Retro / facilitation boardfacilitationStickyNotesBlock + VotingBlock + TimerBlock

14.6 Dynamic data binding

Each block's config_json can declare a data binding — e.g. a BurndownBlock on a team intranet page binds to { team_id: 'X', sprint_id: 'current' }. The block component reads its config, fetches the data, renders. This makes "dynamic intranet pages" trivial: drop a Burndown block, point it at your team, the page is live data.

14.7 Visibility integration

The page_blocks.visibility column uses the same 0/1/2/3 flag as artefact cells. A block hidden at level 1 (product) won't render for users below that level. Comments inside a hidden block are unreachable. The cell-level visibility model unifies cleanly.

14.8 Migration impact

  • Phase 1 ships page_blocks + page_block_comments alongside the artefact tables.
  • Artefact detail pages render through Craft from day one (no separate detail-page UI built and then thrown away).
  • Team intranet, dashboard surfaces follow in Phase 2.
  • Marketplace and PoS+ block packs in Phase 3.

14.9 Block packs as PoS+ revenue

The schema turns block packs into a clean SKU. A pack is a directory of:

  • Manifest JSON declaring blocks, required tier, vertical
  • Compiled React components (or runtime-loaded JS bundles)
  • Optional template forms (couples to the artefact template system)

Customers buy packs, install with one click, the manifest registers blocks and they appear in the page-builder palette. Vector consultancy can author bespoke packs for enterprise customers as a service line.

14. Search & Embedding Architecture

14.1 Hybrid lexical + semantic search

Vector ships hybrid search from Phase 1 — combining Postgres TSVECTOR (lexical, exact-token matching) with pgvector embeddings (semantic, meaning-based matching). The two indexes do different jobs:

Search typeIndexUse case
Exact / keyword / booleanTSVECTOR (Postgres FTS)"find anything with the word CVE-2024-1234", filters, faceting
Semantic / similaritypgvector cosine"find me stories similar to this one", "find AC about payment" — finds rows that mean the same thing without word overlap
HybridCombined ranking (RRF or weighted fusion)The default search bar — fuses both scores for best results

14.2 pgvector overlay

Every _field_values table gains a vector column:

CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector;

ALTER TABLE artefacts_execution_user_stories_field_values
    ADD COLUMN content_embedding vector(384);

CREATE INDEX idx_..._embedding
    ON artefacts_execution_user_stories_field_values
    USING hnsw (content_embedding vector_cosine_ops)
    WHERE archived_at IS NULL;

HNSW over IVFFlat — selected per R012 §A. HNSW gives ~99% recall with default params, self-maintains on insert (no centroid drift, no periodic REINDEX), and avoids IVFFlat's load-first constraint that conflicts with Vector's continuous-write workload. AWS benchmarks show HNSW ~1.5 ms vs IVFFlat ~2.4 ms on 1,536-dim corpora; pgvector 0.7.0 made HNSW builds ~30× faster than 0.5.1, neutralising the historical build-time objection. Partial-index WHERE archived_at IS NULL is mandatory on every embedding index — keeps soft-deleted rows out of recall. Each embedding-bearing row must also carry an embedding_model TEXT column so model migrations can reindex without ambiguity.

Same pattern on every artefact type. Same pattern on the artefact content_plain_text column for the rich-text body. Pure mechanical migration — no special-case logic.

14.3 Why pgvector over standalone vector DBs

  • Same database — no new operational surface, same backups, same transaction model.
  • Full SQL joins — vector search results JOIN to visibility filters in one query: WHERE visibility <= $user_max_visibility AND content_embedding <=> $query_vec < 0.3. With a separate vector DB, this fan-out happens in the application layer.
  • One-line install — Postgres extension, no Docker container, no separate service to monitor.
  • If it bottlenecks later, swap to Qdrant (Apache 2.0, self-hosted) is a backend change, not a schema change.

Standalone vector DBs (Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus, Chroma) are reserved for Phase 3 if/when pgvector hits scale limits. Hosted SaaS (Pinecone, Weaviate Cloud) is excluded by the open-source-first principle (section 2.0).

14.4 Self-hosted embedding service

Embeddings are generated by a self-hosted model service to avoid per-token billing. Two practical paths:

  • Ollama running an embedding model — single Docker container, HTTP API. Recommended for simplicity.
  • Python microservice with sentence-transformers — more bespoke, fully self-controlled.

Default model: nomic-embed-text-v1.5 (Apache 2.0, 768 dims, 8K context). Selected per R012 §B over bge-base-en-v1.5 (512-token context — would silently truncate long acceptance-criteria and retro notes) and all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (smaller dims but lower MTEB score). Nomic-embed scores 62.28 on MTEB vs BGE's 63.55 — within noise — and the Matryoshka representation lets us truncate 768→256 dims later without re-embedding if storage pressure emerges. vector(768) is the column type. Every embedding-bearing row also stores embedding_model TEXT so reindex sweeps after a model upgrade can be staged safely.

Cost goes to zero — CPU/GPU time on existing VPS, no API tokens.

14.5 Async worker pattern (Q9 answer)

Trigger-based synchronous updates would block writes on model inference (~10–100ms per embedding). Solution: async via a durable outbox table with NOTIFY/LISTEN as the wake-up signal (per R012 §D — bare NOTIFY/LISTEN is at-most-once and silently drops messages on connection loss, worker restart, or LISTEN-session long transactions; multiple production incidents are documented at Hasura, Symfony, and Discourse).

-- Outbox queue (durable; survives restarts)
CREATE TABLE search_index_outbox (
    id           BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    table_name   TEXT NOT NULL,
    row_id       UUID NOT NULL,
    enqueued_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    claimed_at   TIMESTAMPTZ,
    attempts     INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0
);
CREATE INDEX idx_outbox_unclaimed ON search_index_outbox (enqueued_at)
    WHERE claimed_at IS NULL;

-- Trigger writes to outbox AND signals via NOTIFY
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION queue_search_index_update() RETURNS trigger AS $$
BEGIN
  INSERT INTO search_index_outbox (table_name, row_id)
    VALUES (TG_TABLE_NAME, NEW.id);
  PERFORM pg_notify('search_index_queue', '');  -- wake-up only
  RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE TRIGGER trg_..._search_queue
    AFTER INSERT OR UPDATE ON artefacts_execution_user_stories_field_values
    FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION queue_search_index_update();

A Go worker process consumes the outbox:

  1. LISTENs on search_index_queue for wake-up signals.
  2. On wake-up (or every 5s as polling fallback), claims a batch with UPDATE ... SET claimed_at=now() WHERE claimed_at IS NULL ... RETURNING * (FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED).
  3. For each claimed row: reads the source row, recomputes search_index TSVECTOR, calls the local Ollama HTTP endpoint to compute content_embedding (~10–100ms), UPDATEs both columns back, then DELETEs the outbox row.
  4. On failure: increment attempts, release claimed_at; dead-letter after N attempts.

This is at-least-once, self-healing across restarts, and survives connection loss. Writes stay snappy (one cheap INSERT plus a payload-less NOTIFY). Index is eventually-consistent, typically <1 second behind. The worker can be scaled horizontally with SKIP LOCKED giving free competitive consumption. Same pattern as Procrastinate, River, neoq, asynq.

14.6 Hybrid search query (illustrative)

-- Reciprocal Rank Fusion of lexical + semantic (Supabase pattern)
-- IMPORTANT: visibility + tenant predicates live INSIDE each CTE.
-- Post-filtering on the final SELECT triggers a 50ms→5s latency cliff
-- (Simon Willison, "The case against pgvector", Nov 2025).
WITH lexical AS (
    SELECT id,
           row_number() OVER (ORDER BY ts_rank(search_index, query) DESC) AS rank
    FROM artefacts_execution_user_stories_field_values, to_tsquery($1) query
    WHERE search_index @@ query
      AND visibility <= $2 AND subscription_id = $3
      AND archived_at IS NULL
    LIMIT 50
),
semantic AS (
    SELECT id,
           row_number() OVER (ORDER BY content_embedding <=> $4::vector) AS rank
    FROM artefacts_execution_user_stories_field_values
    WHERE visibility <= $2 AND subscription_id = $3
      AND archived_at IS NULL
    ORDER BY content_embedding <=> $4::vector
    LIMIT 50
)
SELECT id,
       COALESCE(1.0/(60 + l.rank), 0) + COALESCE(1.0/(60 + s.rank), 0) AS rrf_score
FROM lexical l FULL OUTER JOIN semantic s USING (id)
ORDER BY rrf_score DESC
LIMIT 20;

14.6.1 Defence-in-depth: Postgres RLS on every embedding-bearing table

Per R012 §G — application-layer tenant predicates can be forgotten on a new endpoint, and a forgotten predicate on an embedding-bearing table leaks content across tenants. Every table carrying an embedding column gets a Row-Level Security policy keyed off a session variable set on each connection check-out:

ALTER TABLE artefacts_execution_user_stories_field_values ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation
  ON artefacts_execution_user_stories_field_values
  USING (subscription_id = current_setting('app.current_subscription_id')::uuid);

The application sets SET LOCAL app.current_subscription_id = '...' at the start of each request transaction. A forgotten WHERE clause now returns zero rows instead of leaking. Visibility filtering (the cell-level 0/1/2/3 flag) stays at the application layer because it is per-row contextual, not per-tenant — but the tenant boundary is enforced by the database itself.

14.7 What this unlocks for the future

  • Search-and-compose authoring (section 5) becomes radically better — typing "user can pay" finds AC written as "the customer completes checkout".
  • AI features get a memory layer for free — RAG over the backlog, smart suggestions, similarity search across artefacts, all use the existing index.
  • Template suggestion — "your story is similar to Compliance Story template, want to apply it?".
  • Cross-artefact discovery — "find anything similar to this defect across all 40+ artefact types" works without keyword matching.

Vector becomes architecturally AI-native from Phase 1, even before any visible AI feature ships.

14.8 Vector database as a strategic capability

The vector index is not just a search backend — it is a first-class data layer that compounds in value as more text is written into the system. Every product feature should ask: "is there a vector-powered version of this that's smarter?" Inventory of high-value applications:

  1. Search-and-compose authoring (signature UX, section 5).
  2. Duplicate detection / merge suggestions — embed new artefact, surface top-5 near-duplicates before save.
  3. Smart linking suggestions — "5 stories look related to this defect — link any?" — one-click insert into artefact_links.
  4. Auto-tagging / classification — embed artefact, copy tags from nearest tagged neighbours; system learns team conventions without explicit rules.
  5. Template recommendation — match content against centroids of artefacts grouped by template_form_id, suggest the closest template.
  6. Risk pattern matching — "this story is similar to 3 historical stories that became blocked or missed sprint" — early-warning signal grounded in the team's own history.
  7. Retro insight clustering — embed sticky notes, cluster by similarity to surface themes; replaces manual affinity mapping.
  8. Cross-team knowledge surfacing — "have we written AC for SSO before?" — finds prior work even when terminology has drifted (auth, sign-in, OAuth).
  9. AI agent grounding (RAG) — when AI assistants are bolted on, the existing index is the retrieval layer; no additional infrastructure.
  10. Stakeholder dashboard auto-summaries — "what changed this week?" → embed writes, cluster, generate paragraph per theme.
  11. Onboarding context for new team members — surface the 20 most-similar artefacts to a team's current sprint to orient a new joiner quickly.
  12. Page-builder block recommendations — "users with similar intranet pages added these blocks too" — collaborative filtering on page_block configs.
  13. Template marketplace match-making (PoS+) — "based on your existing artefacts, this Defence template pack is an 87% match".
  14. Field-value reuse mining — periodically cluster _field_values rows; surface clusters to padmins as "20 stories have nearly-identical AC — promote to a reusable snippet?".
  15. Comment thread routing — surface unresolved comments from elsewhere that are about the same topic as the current discussion.

Design principle: every text-bearing surface in Vector gets an embedding column. Semantic search is the default expectation, not an upsell. Embedding columns extend beyond _field_values to:

  • Core artefact tables (one embedding per artefact representing title + content)
  • page_blocks.config_json (text-bearing blocks like markdown, comments)
  • page_block_comments.body (for thread routing)
  • artefact_type_registry.description (template/type matching)
  • doc_pages and doc_code_examples (see section 14.9)

14.9 Documentation as a vector-indexed surface

Vector ships SDK docs, API references, guides, and tutorials that are queryable by meaning from launch. The Samantha SDK docs share the same vector infrastructure as the product itself — one pgvector deployment, one embedding service, one query pattern. Adding semantic doc search costs near-zero because the engine is already running for product features.

14.9.1 Schema

CREATE TABLE doc_pages (
    id                  UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    slug                TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL,           -- 'samantha/portfolio/find'
    section             TEXT NOT NULL,                  -- 'sdk' | 'api' | 'guide' | 'tutorial'
    title               TEXT NOT NULL,
    content             JSONB,                          -- Lexical state
    content_plain_text  TEXT,                           -- denormalised for FTS
    content_embedding   vector(384),                    -- semantic search
    sdk_method          TEXT,                           -- 'samantha.portfolio.find'
    version             TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'latest',
    visibility          SMALLINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 3,    -- docs typically public
    published_at        TIMESTAMPTZ,
    archived_at         TIMESTAMPTZ,
    created_at          TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    updated_at          TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE doc_code_examples (
    id                  UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    doc_page_id         UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES doc_pages(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    language            TEXT NOT NULL,                  -- 'typescript' | 'go' | 'curl'
    code                TEXT NOT NULL,
    description         TEXT,
    code_embedding      vector(384),                    -- semantic match on code intent
    position            INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0
);

14.9.2 Doc-specific use cases

  1. Semantic doc search — "how do I check if a user can edit a portfolio" finds the right method even though the doc says "permission verification for portfolio artefacts".
  2. Method discovery from intent — "create a new story with custom fields" → samantha.execution.userStories.create() with code snippet.
  3. AI-powered docs assistant (RAG over docs) — Stripe-style / Vercel-style chat assistant grounded in the official docs.
  4. Cross-reference between docs and live code — "this story relates to samantha.portfolio.find() — link the docs?".
  5. Code-comment to doc-page suggestion — embed JSDoc/Go-doc comments, suggest existing methods to prevent API surface drift.
  6. Versioned doc similarity — auto-changelog by clustering diffs between SDK versions.
  7. Support deflection — embed customer questions, search docs + prior tickets, suggest answers before submission (Stripe reports ~40% support load reduction).
  8. Tutorial path generation — "I want to build a Slack integration" → ordered learning path across docs, examples, Q&A.
  9. Multilingual docs (eventual) — cross-lingual embedding models let non-English developers search in their native language.
  10. SDK-aware chat for AI agents — customer agents using Samantha can vector-search the docs at runtime to write correct code; Vector becomes AI-agent-friendly by default.

14.9.3 Page-builder integration

Doc pages render through the same Craft.js engine (section 13) with host_kind = 'doc_page'. New block types: MethodSignatureBlock (SDK spec with try-it-out), CodeExampleBlock (multi-language with copy button), ParameterTableBlock (args/props table), RelatedDocsBlock (auto-populated by semantic similarity to current page). One product, one rendering layer, one search engine.
